name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلدِّينَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْإِسْلَـٰمُ ۗ وَمَا ٱخْتَلَفَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْعِلْمُ بَغْيًۢا بَيْنَهُمْ ۗ وَمَن يَكْفُرْ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ (١٩)
```

```english
Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam, and those who were given the Book did not differ except after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves. And whoever denies Allah’s signs [should know that] Allah is swift at reckoning. (19)
```

```arabic
فَإِنْ حَآجُّوكَ فَقُلْ أَسْلَمْتُ وَجْهِىَ لِلَّهِ وَمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَنِ ۗ وَقُل لِّلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْأُمِّيِّـۧنَ ءَأَسْلَمْتُمْ ۚ فَإِنْ أَسْلَمُوا۟ فَقَدِ ٱهْتَدَوا۟ ۖ وَّإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَإِنَّمَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بَصِيرٌۢ بِٱلْعِبَادِ (٢٠)
```

```english
If they argue with you, say, ‘I have submitted my will to Allah, and [so has] he who follows me.’ And say to those who were given the Book and the uninstructed ones, ‘Do you submit?’ If they submit, they will certainly be guided; but if they turn away, then your duty is only to communicate, and Allah watches His servants. (20)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَكْفُرُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَقْتُلُونَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ وَيَقْتُلُونَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ مِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ فَبَشِّرْهُم بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (٢١)
```

```english
Those who deny Allah’s signs and kill the prophets unjustly and kill those who call for justice from among the people, inform them of a painful punishment. (21)
```

```arabic
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَمَا لَهُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ (٢٢)
```

```english
They are the ones whose works have failed in this world and the Hereafter, and they will have no helpers. (22)
```

```arabic
أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ نَصِيبًا مِّنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ يُدْعَوْنَ إِلَىٰ كِتَـٰبِ ٱللَّهِ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَهُمْ ثُمَّ يَتَوَلَّىٰ فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُمْ وَهُم مُّعْرِضُونَ (٢٣)
```

```english
Have you not regarded those who were given a share of the Book, who are summoned to the Book of Allah in order that it may judge between them, whereat a part of them refuse to comply and they are disregardful? (23)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَالُوا۟ لَن تَمَسَّنَا ٱلنَّارُ إِلَّآ أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَٰتٍ ۖ وَغَرَّهُمْ فِى دِينِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (٢٤)
```

```english
That is because they say, ‘The Fire shall not touch us except for a number of days,’ and they have been misled in their religion by what they used to fabricate. (24)
```

```arabic
فَكَيْفَ إِذَا جَمَعْنَـٰهُمْ لِيَوْمٍ لَّا رَيْبَ فِيهِ وَوُفِّيَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّا كَسَبَتْ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (٢٥)
```

```english
But how will it be [with them] when We gather them on a day in which there is no doubt, and every soul shall be recompensed fully for what it has earned, and they will not be wronged? (25)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses address the state of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), who are the last of the three groups previously mentioned as subjects of discussion in this Surah. The most significant among them, in terms of the primary intent of the discourse, are the People of the Book from the Jews and the Christians; for it is regarding them and their affairs that the bulk of the Surah was revealed and to them it returns.

**His saying, the Exalted:** إن الدين عند الله الإسلام (Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam). The linguistic meaning of Islam (الإسلام) — *submission and surrender* — has already been discussed. It appears that this meaning is what is intended here, given the context of what He mentions regarding the disagreement of the People of the Book after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves. Thus, the meaning would be: Indeed, religion with Allah, the Glorified, is one; there is no disagreement in it. He did not command His servants except with it, nor did He clarify for them in what He revealed of the Book to His prophets except it, nor did He establish the indicative signs (الآيات) except for it. This is Islam, which is submission (التسليم) to the truth—the truth of belief and the truth of action. In other words, it is submission to the clarification (البيان) issued from the station of Lordship (مقام الربوبية) regarding divine gnosis (المعارف) and legal rulings (الأحكام). Although it may vary in quantity and quality across the laws (شرائع) of His prophets and messengers—as Allah, the Glorified, relates in His Book—it is in reality nothing but a single matter. The differences between the laws are matters of perfection (الكمال) and deficiency (النقص), rather than contradiction (التضاد) or incompatibility (التنافي); the distinction between them is one of degrees. What unites them all is that they constitute submission and obedience to Allah, the Glorified, in what He desires from His servants as conveyed by the tongues of His messengers.

This, then, is the religion that Allah desired for His servants and clarified for them. Its necessary implication is that a human being should adopt what has been clarified to him of its gnosis with true clarity, and pause at ambiguities (الشبهات) with the stance of submission, without exercising personal discretion (تصرف) therein. As for the disagreement of the People of the Book—the Jews and the Christians—concerning religion despite the revelation of the Divine Book to them and His clarification of what constitutes religion with Him (which is submission to Him), it did not arise from ignorance on their part regarding the reality of the matter or the fact that religion is one. Rather, they were knowledgeable of that; it was only their envy (بغي) and injustice (ظلم) that drove them to it without excuse. This constituted disbelief (كفر) on their part in the signs of Allah (آيات الله) which clarified the truth and reality of the matter to them—not disbelief in Allah Himself, for they acknowledge Him. And whoever disbelieves in the signs of Allah, then indeed Allah is swift at reckoning (سريع الحساب); He reckons with him swiftly in his worldly life and the Hereafter. In this world, it is through disgrace and the stripping away of the happiness of life; in the Hereafter, it is through the painful punishment of the Fire.

The evidence for the general swiftness of reckoning encompassing both this world and the Hereafter is His saying, the Exalted, two verses later: **أولئك الذين حبطت أعمالهم في الدنيا والآخرة وما لهم من ناصرين** — "They are the ones whose works have come to naught in this world and the Hereafter, and they will have no helpers." (3:22)

From what has preceded, it becomes clear, **firstly:** that the meaning of religion being "with Allah" (عند الله) and its presence before Him, the Glorified, is a legislative presence (الحضور التشريعي). This means it is a single law (شرع) that does not differ except in degrees and according to the capacities of different nations, rather than it being one in terms of creation (التكوين)—meaning it being a single thing deposited in the human primordial nature (الفطرة) in a uniform manner.

**Secondly:** that the intended meaning of "the signs" (الآيات) is the signs of revelation (آيات الوحي) and the divine clarifications He bestowed upon His prophets, rather than the ontological signs (الآيات التكوينية) indicating Oneness (الوحدانية) and the accompanying divine gnosis.

The verse contains a threat to the People of the Book with that which is necessitated by envy (البغي), which is retribution (الانتقام), just as His saying, the Exalted, in the preceding verses: **قل للذين كفروا ستغلبون وتحشرون إلى جهنم** — "Say to the faithless: ‘You shall be overcome and mustered toward hell’" (3:12) contains a threat to the polytheists and disbelievers. Perhaps this is why He grouped the People of the Book and the polytheists together in the following verse in the address: **قل للذين أوتوا الكتاب والأميين أأسلمتم** — "Say to those who were given the Book and the unlettered: ‘Have you submitted?’" (3:20). This also carries an intimation of a threat.

**His saying, the Exalted:** فإن حاجوك فقل أسلمت وجهي لله ومن اتبعن (If they argue with you, say, ‘I have submitted my whole self to Allah, and [so has] he who follows me.’) The pronoun in "they argue with you" (حاجوك) refers to the People of the Book, which is apparent. What is intended is their arguing regarding the matter of disagreement, by saying: "Our disagreement is not due to envy on our part after clarification; rather, it is something our intellects, understandings, and our exertion (اجتهاد) in seeking knowledge of the realities of religion have led us to, without us abandoning submission to the side of the Truth, the Glorified. What you see and call toward, O Muhammad, is of this same nature," or they might say something similar. The evidence for this is His saying: "Say: I have submitted my whole self to Allah," and His saying: "And say to those who were given the Book and the unlettered: ‘Have you submitted?’" For these two sentences are arguments (حجة) brought forth to cut off their contention and disputation, not a turning away from arguing with them.

Its meaning, while maintaining its connection to what preceded it, is: Religion with Allah is Islam; the Books of Allah do not differ regarding it, nor does a sound intellect doubt it. It follows from this that there is no argument against you in your submission while you are a submitter (مسلم). If they argue with you regarding the matter of religion, then say: "I have submitted my whole self to Allah, and so has he who follows me." This is the religion, and there is no argument after religion in the matter of religion. Then ask them: Have they submitted? If they submit, they are rightly guided, and they must accept what Allah revealed to you and to those before you; there is no argument against them, and no contention between you after that. But if they turn away, do not contend with them or argue with them, for contention is not appropriate in a necessary (ضروري) matter—which is that religion is submission to Allah, the Glorified—and your duty is only to deliver the message.

The Glorified has joined the People of the Book and the unlettered (الاميين) in the verse by saying: "And say to those who were given the Book and the unlettered: ‘Have you submitted?’" because religion is common to them all, even if they differ in monotheism (التوحيد) and polytheism (التشريك).

Submission (الإسلام) was linked to the "face" (الوجه)—which is that part of a thing that meets you, or the face in the specific sense, because the submission of the face, as it contains most of the senses and perceptions, constitutes a submission of the entire body—to indicate the meaning of turning toward and humbling oneself (الخضوع) to the command of the Lord, the Exalted. The phrase "and he who follows me" (ومن اتبعن) was added to preserve the station of followership and as an honor for the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وقل للذين أوتوا الكتاب والأميين أأسلمتم (And say to those who were given the Book and the unlettered: ‘Have you submitted?’) until the end of the verse. What is intended by "the unlettered" (الأميين) is the polytheists; they were called this because those placed in opposition to them were called "the People of the Book." Likewise, the People of the Book used to call them this, as related by the Exalted in His saying: **ليس علينا في الأميين سبيل** — "There is no blame upon us in respect of the unlettered" (3:75). The unlettered (الأمي) is one who neither writes nor reads.

In His saying, the Exalted: **وإن تولوا فإنما عليك البلاغ والله بصير بالعباد** — "But if they turn away, then your duty is only to communicate; and Allah sees best the servants" (3:20), there is an indication:

**Firstly:** of the prohibition against sophistry (المراء) and persistence in disputation; for arguing with one who denies a necessary truth (الضروري) is nothing but sophistry and obstinacy in discussion.

**Secondly:** that the judgment regarding people and the command (الأمر) belongs entirely to Allah, the Glorified. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم is nothing but a messenger who delivers, not a dominating ruler, as the Exalted said: **ليس لك من الأمر شيء** — "You have no hand in the matter" (3:128), and He said: **لست عليهم بمصيطر** — "You are not a taskmaster over them" (88:22).

**Thirdly:** of a threat to the People of the Book and the polytheists; for concluding the speech with His saying: "and Allah sees best the servants," after His saying: "your duty is only to communicate," is not devoid of this. This is supported by the threat that occurs in a similar verse, which is His saying, the Exalted: **قولوا آمنا بالله** — "Say, ‘We have faith in Allah...’" until He says: **ونحن له مسلمون فإن آمنوا بمثل ما آمنتم به فقد اهتدوا وإن تولوا فإنما هم في شقاق فسيكفيكهم الله وهو السميع العليم** — "and to Him we submit. So if they believe in the like of what you believe in, then they are certainly guided; and if they turn away, then they are only [steeped] in defiance; so Allah will suffice you against them, and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing" (2:136-137). The verse mentions that if the People of the Book turn away from Islam, they are persisting in opposition; then He threatens them with that which consoles the Prophet and puts his mind at ease. Thus, the verse—meaning His saying: "But if they turn away, then your duty is only to communicate"—is an allusion (كناية) to the command to leave them to their Lord and return their affair to Him; He sees best His servants and will judge them according to what their state necessitates and what the tongue of their capacity (لسان استعدادهم) asks of Him.

From here it becomes clear that what some commentators have mentioned—that the verse contains evidence for freedom of belief (حرية الاعتقاد) in the matter of religion and that there is no compulsion in it—is not well-founded; for the verse, as you have seen, is directed toward something else.

In His saying: "sees best the servants" (بصير بالعباد), where He employed the title of "servitude" (العبودية) and did not say: "sees best them" or "sees best the people" and the like, there is an intimation that His judgment is effective (نافذ) over them and will pass upon them; for they are His servants and subjects of His Lordship (مربوبون), whether they submit or turn away.

**His saying, the Exalted:** إن الذين يكفرون بآيات الله (Indeed, those who deny the signs of Allah) until the end of the verse. Although the speech in the verse is presented as a new beginning (استيناف), it is nevertheless not devoid of an intimation and clarification of the threat suggested by the end of the previous verse; for its content applies to the People of the Book, especially the Jews.

His saying: "they deny" (يكفرون) and "they kill" (يقتلون) in two places are in the present/continuous tense to indicate continuity (الاستمرار). They indicate that disbelief in the signs of Allah—which is disbelief after clarification—is envy (بغي), and the killing of prophets—which is killing without right—and the killing of those who call to equity (القسط) and justice and forbid injustice and envy, was a habit and a prevailing custom among them, as contained in the history of the Jews. They killed a great many and a vast multitude of their prophets and their servants who commanded the right and forbade the wrong; likewise, the Christians followed in their footsteps.

His saying: **فبشرهم بعذاب أليم** — "so give them the news of a painful punishment" (3:21) is an explicit statement of the encompassing of wrath (الغضب) and the descent of displeasure (السخط). It is not merely the punishment of the Hereafter, as evidenced by His saying, the Exalted, immediately following the verse: **أولئك الذين حبطت أعمالهم في الدنيا والآخرة** — "They are the ones whose works have come to naught in this world and the Hereafter" (3:22). Thus, they are given news of both worldly and otherworldly punishment. As for the otherworldly, it is the painful punishment of the Fire; as for the worldly, it is what they encountered of slaughter, expulsion, and the loss of wealth and lives, and Allah’s displeasure upon them by casting enmity and hatred among them until the Day of Resurrection, as the verses of the Mighty Book explicitly state.

In His saying, the Exalted: **أولئك الذين حبطت أعمالهم في الدنيا والآخرة وما لهم من ناصرين** — "They are the ones whose works have come to naught in this world and the Hereafter, and they will have no helpers" (3:22), there is an indication, **firstly:** of the nullification (حبط) of the deeds of one who kills a man because of his commanding the right or forbidding the wrong. **Secondly:** of the fact that intercession (الشفاعة) will not encompass him on the Day of Resurrection, due to His saying: "and they will have no helpers."

**His saying, the Exalted:** ألم تر إلى الذين أوتوا نصيباً من الكتاب (Have you not regarded those who were given a share of the Book?) until the end of the verse. This points toward the recording of envy (البغي) against the People of the Book according to what Allah the Exalted attributed to them: that they act with envy by adopting opposition and creating disagreement of word in religion. For when they are called to the judgment of the Book—the Book of Allah—between them, they do not submit to it, but turn away and disregard it. This is nothing but due to their being deluded by their saying: "The Fire shall not touch us..." and by what they fabricated against Allah in their religion.

What is intended by "those who were given a share of the Book" (الذين أوتوا نصيباً من الكتاب) is the People of the Book. It did not say: "were given the Book," but rather "were given a share of the Book" to indicate that what is in their hands of the Book is only a portion of it, not all of it. This is because their distortion (تحريف), alteration, and tampering with the Book of Allah caused many of its parts to vanish, as intimated by His saying at the end of the following verse: **وغرهم في دينهم ما كانوا يفترون** — "and they were deluded in their religion by what they used to fabricate" (3:24). In any case, the meaning—and Allah knows best—is that they turn away from the judgment of the Book of Allah, feeling empowered by what they said and deluded by what they fabricated from themselves, sufficing themselves with it over the Book.

**His saying, the Exalted:** ذلك بأنهم قالوا لن تمسنا النار (That is because they say, ‘The Fire shall not touch us...’) Its meaning is clear. Their being deluded by the fabrication that their own souls fabricated—even though a human being is not deceived by his own self when he knows it is a false deception—is because the deluded ones are other than the fabricators. Based on this, the attribution of the fabrication—which their predecessors resorted to—to these deluded ones among the successors is because they are a single nation, some of whom are pleased with the deeds of others.

Alternatively, it is because being deluded by the delusion of the self and being deluded by a false fabrication despite knowing it is a false fabrication—and the deluded one remembering that he is the one who fabricated the very lie he is deluded by—is not far-fetched for the People of the Book, and for the Jews in particular. Allah has related from them the like of this, indeed something even more amazing than that, where the Exalted said: **وإذا لقوا الذين آمنوا قالوا آمنا وإذا خلا بعضهم إلى بعض قالوا أتحدثونهم بما فتح الله عليكم ليحاجوكم به عند ربكم أفلا تعقلون أولا يعلمون أن الله يعلم ما يسرون وما يعلنون** — "When they meet the faithful, they say, ‘We believe,’ and when they are alone with one another, they say, ‘Do you discourse to them of what Allah has disclosed to you, so that they may use it as an argument against you before your Lord? Do you not exercise your reason?’ Do they not know that Allah knows whatever they hide and whatever they disclose?" (2:76-77).

Furthermore, a human being acts in his deeds and actions according to the states (الأحوال) or psychological traits (الملكات النفسانية) that have formed within him, and the images that his soul has beautified and adorned for him, rather than according to what he has acquired knowledge of. Just as one accustomed to using harmful substances like opium, tobacco, eating clay, and the like, uses them while knowing they are harmful and that using what is harmful is inappropriate; however, the form (الهيئة) established in his soul is pleasurable to him and attracts him toward use, leaving him no room for reflection or avoidance. Examples of this are many.

Thus, due to the entrenchment of pride (الكبر), envy (البغي), and the love of desires in their souls, they act according to what their fabrication calls them to. So their fabrication was what deluded them in their religion. Moreover, they repeatedly mentioned what they fabricated against Allah, the Glorified, and continued to repeat it and instill it into themselves until they became convinced of it—that is, they found tranquility and relied upon it through the instillation (التلقين) that produces the effect of knowledge, as explained by psychologists. Thus, the false fabrication, through repetition and instillation, came to delude them in their religion and prevent them from submitting to Allah and humbling themselves to the truth He revealed in His Book.

**His saying, the Exalted:** فكيف إذا جمعناهم ليوم لا ريب فيه (But how will it be when We gather them for a day in which there is no doubt?) until the end of the verse. The object of "how" (كيف) is elided, indicated by the context, such as "what will they do" or similar. The verse contains a threat to those who turned away when called to the Book of Allah to judge between them while they were disregarding it. However, since the intent was to clarify that they cannot frustrate Allah, the Glorified, the discourse took from their state on the Day of Resurrection—when they will be submissive—that which contrasts with their state in this world when called to the judgment of the Book of Allah while they were not submitting to it and were arrogant toward it. For this reason, the two statements were placed in parallel, and what will happen to them on the Day of Resurrection was expressed with the like of His saying: "when We gather them for a day in which there is no doubt," rather than saying: "when We give them life" or "resurrect them" or similar.

The meaning—and Allah knows best—is that they turn away and disregard when called to the Book of Allah to judge between them, out of delusion by what they fabricated in their religion and arrogance toward the truth. So how will they act when We gather them for a day in which there is no doubt—which is the Day of the Decisive Judgment and the True Decree—and every soul is paid in full for what it has earned, and the judgment is a judgment of justice and they shall not be wronged? If this is the case, it was incumbent upon them not to turn away and disregard, manifesting thereby that they can frustrate Allah or overcome His command; for all power belongs to Allah, and these are but days of respite and trial.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, narrated from Muhammad ibn Muslim, who said: "I asked him about His saying: إن الدين عند الله الإسلام (Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam). He said: 'That which contains faith (الإيمان).'"

And from Ibn Shahrashub, from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: إن الدين عند الله الإسلام (Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam), the verse; he said: "Submission (التسليم) to Ali ibn Abi Talib with the guardianship (الولاية)."

**I say:** This is of the nature of application (الجري); perhaps that is also what was intended by the previous narration.

And also from him, from Imam Ali (peace be upon him), who said: "I shall certainly attribute to Islam an attribution that no one before me has attributed, and no one after me shall attribute: Islam is submission (التسليم), and submission is certainty (اليقين), and certainty is confirmation (التصديق), and confirmation is affirmation (الإقرار), and affirmation is fulfillment (الأداء), and fulfillment is action (العمل). The believer takes his religion from his Lord; indeed, the believer is known by his faith in his action, and indeed the disbeliever is known by his disbelief through his denial.

O people! Your religion, your religion! For a bad deed (السيئة) within it is better than a good deed (الحسنة) in other than it. Indeed, a bad deed within it is forgiven, and indeed a good deed in other than it is not accepted."

**I say:** His (peace be upon him) saying: "I shall certainly attribute to Islam an attribution," what is intended by "attribution" (النسبة) is definition (التعريف), just as Surah al-Tawhid is called in the reports "the attribution of the Lord" (نسبة الرب). That which he defined it with is a definition by necessary consequence (اللازم) in other than the first, meaning his saying: "Islam is submission," for that is a verbal definition in which a term is defined by another term clearer than it. It is possible that what is intended by Islam is its technical meaning—which is this religion brought by Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم—referring to His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam," and by submission (التسليم) is meant humility and obedience in essence and action; thus everything returns to definition by necessary consequence.

The meaning is: This religion called Islam entails the human being's submission to Allah, the Glorified, in essence and action, and his placing himself and his deeds under His command and will—which is submission. Submission to Allah entails or necessitates certainty (اليقين) in Allah and the removal of doubt regarding Him. Certainty entails confirmation (التصديق) and manifesting the truth of the religion. Confirmation entails affirmation (الإقرار), which is the conviction of its stability and its being firm, not wavering in its place nor departing from its station. Its affirmation entails its fulfillment (الأداء), and its fulfillment entails action (العمل).

His (peace be upon him) saying: "And indeed a good deed in other than it is not accepted," what is intended by "non-acceptance" is the absence of reward in exchange for it in the Hereafter, or the absence of a beautiful, praised effect with Allah in this world through the happiness of life and in the Hereafter through the bliss of Paradise. This does not contradict what has been narrated that disbelievers are rewarded for their good deeds with something of the good things of this world; the Exalted said: **فمن يعمل مثقال ذرة خيراً يره** — "So whoever does an atom’s weight of good will see it" (99:7).

In *al-Majma'*, from Abu Ubaydah al-Jarrah, who said: "I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, which of the people will have the most severe punishment on the Day of Resurrection?' He said: 'A man who killed a prophet, or a man who commanded the right or forbade the wrong.' Then he recited: **الذين يقتلون النبيين بغير حق ويقتلون الذين يأمرون بالقسط من الناس** (those who kill the prophets without right and kill those who call to equity from among the people). Then he said: 'O Abu Ubaydah, the Children of Israel killed forty-three prophets in one hour. Then one hundred and twelve men from the worshippers of the Children of Israel stood up and commanded those who killed them with the right and forbade them from the wrong, and they were all killed at the end of that day. That is what Allah mentioned.'"

**I say:** This meaning was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn Jarir and Ibn Abi Hatim from Abu Ubaydah.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ibn Ishaq, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم entered the house of study (بيت المدراس) upon a group of Jews and called them to Allah. Nu'man ibn Amr and Harith ibn Zayd said to him: 'Upon what religion are you, O Muhammad?' He said: 'Upon the creed (ملة) of Abraham and his religion.' They said: 'But Abraham was a Jew.' The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم said to them: 'Then come to the Torah, for it is between us and you.' They refused him, so Allah revealed: **ألم تر إلى الذين أوتوا نصيباً من الكتاب يدعون إلى كتاب الله ليحكم بينهم** (Have you not regarded those who were given a share of the Book, being invited to the Book of Allah so that it may judge between them...) until His saying: **وغرهم في دينهم ما كانوا يفترون** (and they were deluded in their religion by what they used to fabricate)."

**I say:** Some have narrated that His saying, the Exalted: "Have you not regarded" (ألم تر) was revealed regarding the story of stoning (الرجم), and its mention will come in the discussion following His saying, the Exalted: **يا أهل الكتاب قد جاءكم رسولنا يبين لكم كثيراً مما كنتم تخفون من الكتاب** — "O People of the Book! Certainly Our Apostle has come to you, clarifying for you much of what you used to hide of the Book..." (5:15). Both narrations are solitary reports (آحاد) and are not of great strength.
